---
title: "Project 1"
author: "Yuan Xie"
format: "html"
embed-resources: True
---

# 1 Dogs Data\
## 1.1 Image of dogs\
Search for an image of dogs and include it (i.e. insert it) in your document.\
![figure](dogs.jpg)
\

## 1.2 Vectors\
Consider the following data about 10 dog breeds\
![figure](data.jpg)
Create vectors and factors for the columns in the data table displayed above, according to the following data types. Encode missing values as ```NA```. BTW: You **cannot** use any of the explicit coercion functions: e.g. ```as.integer()```, ```as.logical()```, etc.\

- ```breed```: character vector\
- ```avg_life```: integer vector (expectancy life in years)\
- ```energy```: factor (energy level); hint: ```factor()``` is your friend\
- ```trainability```: double or real vector (trainability level)\
- ```friendly```: logical vector (tends to be very friendly)\

```{r}
# breed: character vector
breed <- c("Beagle", "Bulldog", "Collie", "German Shepherd",
           "Golden Retriever", "Greyhound", "Labrador Retriever",
           "Pomeranian", "Pug", "Shiba Inu")
breed

# avg_life: integer vector
avg_life <- c(12L, 9L, 13L, 9L, 11L, 12L, 11L, 14L, 14L, 15L)
avg_life

# energy: factor
energy <- factor(c("high", "medium", "medium", "medium", "very high",
                   "high", "very high", "medium", "medium", "medium"))
energy

# trainability: double (real) vector
trainability <- c(0.6, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0, 1.0, NA, 1.0, 0.6, 0.6, 0.4)
trainability

# friendly: logical vector
friendly <- c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)
friendly
```

## 1.3 Subscripting (aka Indexing, Slicing)\
Use **bracket** notation to write R commands—displaying the output—that answer the following questions. Answer each part in its individual code chunk.\
\
a) What is the breed with the longest average life expectancy diameter? Hint: the function ```which.max()``` is your friend.
```{r}
breed[which.max(avg_life)]
```
\
b) How many breeds don’t tend to be very friendly? Hint: the function ```sum()``` is your friend.
```{r}
sum(friendly == FALSE)
```
\
c) What is the median trainability value of those breeds that are friendly?
```{r}
median(trainability[friendly == TRUE], na.rm = TRUE)
```
\
d) How many breeds have average life expectancy values smaller than the median average life expectancy?
```{r}
sum(avg_life < median(avg_life))
```
\
e) How many breeds have medium energy level and tend to be very friendly?
```{r}
sum(energy == "medium" & friendly == TRUE)
```
\
f) What breeds have medium energy level and tend to be very friendly?
```{r}
breed[energy == "medium" & friendly == TRUE]
```
\
g) What is the minimum trainability value for those breeds with an average lifetime of 13 years or more? Hint: Pay attention to ```NA``` values.
```{r}
min(trainability[avg_life >= 13], na.rm = TRUE)
```
\

# 2 Vectorized Functions\
For each function or operator listed below, state whether the function/operator is vectorized and provide a code example to support your claim.\

1. ```length()```: False. It just returns the length of a vector, which is a single value. It does not calculate each element one by one.
```{r}
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
length(x)
```

2. ```*```: True. It multiplies each element of a vector by another vector or a scalar.
```{r}
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
x * 2
```

3. ```log()```: True. It applies the logarithmic function to each element of the vector.
```{r}
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
log(x)
```

4. ```max()```: False. It changes the vector to the single largest value rather than operating element-wise.
```{r}
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
max(x)
```

5. ```trunc()```: True. This function actually rounds each number towards zero one by one.
```{r}
x <- c(1.1, 2.6, 3.2, 4.8)
trunc(x)
```
\

# 3 Loan Amortization Payment Formula\
Consider the following formula for computing the periodic payment (PMT) of a loan amortization:、
$$
\begin{equation}
\text{PMT} = P \times 
\frac{\frac{r}{k}\left(1 + \frac{r}{k}\right)^{nk}}
{\left(1 + \frac{r}{k}\right)^{nk} - 1}
\end{equation}
$$
where:\
- P = principal (loan amount)\
- PMT = periodic payment (typically monthly)\
- $r$ = interest rate\
- $n$ = number of years\
- $k$ = number of periodic payments (typically 12: monthly)\
\
**What it is**: when $k$ = 12, this formula gives you the monthly payment of a loan amortization.\
**How to use it**: calculate the fixed periodic—usually monthly—payment of a loan.\
**Best for**: Amortization is used in personal loan, home loan (mortgage), auto loan, student loan repayment schedule preparation.\

## 3.1 Computing Payment\
Write R code to answer the following question.\
\
Suppose that one of your friends recently graduated from a College in California with $45,000 in student loan debt, at an interest rate of 5.25%. What would be the monthly payment if your friend wants to pay-off their student loan in 20 years?
```{r}
# input the data
P <- 45000
r <- 0.0525
n <- 20
k <- 12

# plug these data into the function provided
PMT <- P * ( (r / k) * (1 + r / k)^(n * k) ) /
              ( (1 + r / k)^(n * k) - 1 )

PMT
```
**Interpretation**: The monthly payment should be 303.2299 dollars.\

## 3.2 Computing Years\
Write R code to answer the following question.\
\
Your friend has various monthly payments to make such as rent, car loan, cell-phone, internet, streaming services, etc. With so many things to pay for, your friend is thinking of making monthly payments of \$220 to pay for their student loan. How many years would it take your friend to be student-loan debt free if they plan to make monthly payments of \$220? _Hint_: use a trial-and-error process for various values of $n$ to find the answer.
```{r}
# input the data
P <- 45000
r <- 0.0525
k <- 12
expected_PMT <- 220

n_vals <- seq(10, 60, by = 0.1)

# calculate all the corresponding PMT values
PMT_vals <- P * ( (r / k) * (1 + r / k)^(n_vals * k) ) /
                  ( (1 + r / k)^(n_vals * k) - 1 )
# find the number of year with the smallest difference
n_vals[which.min(abs(PMT_vals - expected_PMT))]
```
**Interpretation**: It will take 43 years.\

# 4 Future Value of Ordinary Annuity\
Mrs. Savingswood wants to save money for the college education of her 3 year-old daughter. She plans to deposit \$100 at the end of each month, for the next 15 years, into a mutual fund that gives—on average—a 10% annual rate of return. How much money will Mrs. Savingswood have at the end of 15 years?\
\
We can answer this question with the Future Value of an Ordinary Annuity formula:\
$$
\text{FV ordinary annuity} \;\longrightarrow\;
\text{FV} = \text{PMT} \times 
\frac{\left(1 + \frac{r}{k}\right)^{nk} - 1}{r/k}
$$
where:\
- FV = future value (amount accumulated)\
- PMT = periodic contribution made at the end of each period\
- $r$ = annual interest rate (i.e. annual rate of return)\
- $n$ = number of years\
- $k$ = number of compounding periods per year; e.g. yearly ($k$ = 1), twice yearly ($k$ = 2), quarterly ($k$ = 4) and monthly ($k$ = 12).\
\
Notice that if $r$ = 0 then:
$$ \text{FV} = \text{PMT} \times nk$$

## 4.1 Function ```annuity()```\
- Based on the formula from equation 2, write a function ```annuity()```, that allows you to compute the **future value of an ordinary annuity**.\
- Make sure your function works with $r$ = 0\
- Give descriptive names to the arguments of the function.\
- Likewise, give default values to the arguments.\
- Don’t forget to include comments to document the arguments of the function.\
```{r}
# set the function with default parameters
annuity <- function(payment = 100,
                    annual_rate = 0.05,
                    years = 10,
                    periods_per_year = 12) {

  # total number of payments
  ## nk
  n_periods <- years * periods_per_year 
  
  # handle the case where interest rate is zero
  if (annual_rate == 0) {
    ## FV = PMT * nk
    FV <- payment * n_periods 
  } else {
    ## r/k
    rate_per_period <- annual_rate / periods_per_year
    ## plug into the whole formula
    FV <- payment * ((1 + rate_per_period)^n_periods - 1) / rate_per_period
  }
  
  return(FV)
}
```

## 4.2  Using ```annuity()```\
Use your function ```annuity()``` to find how much money Mrs. Savingswood will have at the end of 15 years.
```{r}
# just plugging the parameters into the defined function
annuity(payment = 100,
         annual_rate = 0.10,
         years = 15,
         periods_per_year = 12)
```
**Interpretation**: Mrs. Savingswood will have 41447.03 dollars.
